clinicalProblem.clinicalIntent: Exclude white-coat effect in apparent resistant hypertension using out-of-office BP monitoring
clinicalProblem.whenToUse: Initial work-up of apparent resistant hypertension
clinicalProblem.decisionOrAction: Recommend out-of-office BP monitoring to differentiate white-coat effect from true resistant hypertension

navigationPath.purpose: Entry point for resistant hypertension evaluation
Review elevated office BP readings despite ≥3 medications
Determine white-coat effect vs true resistant hypertension

validationRules: Office BP must be elevated despite ≥3 antihypertensive medications
Out-of-office BP requires minimum 14 readings for ABPM or 7 days for HBPM

rules: Compare office BP with out-of-office BP
If out-of-office BP is controlled (<130/80) while office BP elevated → white-coat effect
If out-of-office BP is also elevated (≥130/80) → true resistant hypertension
White-coat effect is NOT associated with increased CVD risk
Higher out-of-office BP in apparent resistant hypertension IS associated with increased CVD risk, independent of office BP

recommendation.description: Class 2a: 'It is reasonable to exclude white-coat effect using out-of-office BP monitoring'

thresholds.sbp: N/A - not applicable for this recommendation

nextStepRouting.providerSuggestion: If white-coat effect: continue current medications, avoid intensification. If true resistant hypertension: consider adding mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist or refer to specialist
nextStepRouting.followUpTiming: Repeat out-of-office BP monitoring annually or with medication changes
nextStepRouting.repeatMeasurement: Confirm with repeat ABPM or HBPM if initial results equivocal
nextStepRouting.navigatorEscalation: Flag true resistant hypertension for specialist referral
nextStepRouting.documentation: Log office vs out-of-office BP comparison and final classification
